report_metadata.cancer_type: Laryngeal Small Cell Carcinoma â€” Neuroendocrine (Pulmonary-Type SCNEC/LCNEC)

treatments.name: Cisplatin + Etoposide (PE, 4-6 Cycles â€” SCLC Paradigm)

evidence_level.rationale: Cisplatin-etoposide is the established standard-of-care chemotherapy for small cell carcinoma regardless of primary site, based on decades of SCLC randomized trial evidence (Evans et al. JCO 1985, Roth et al. JCO 1992). NCCN Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors Guidelines 2025 explicitly recommend treating laryngeal SCNEC with PE chemotherapy identical to SCLC paradigm, supported by WHO 2022 classification that recognizes extrapulmonary SCNEC as biologically equivalent to pulmonary SCLC. This is Level 1 evidence by direct application of SCLC standard of care.

survival_benefit.rationale: Roth et al. JCO 1992 (N=437 extensive SCLC) demonstrated PE achieved response rates of 61% and median OS of 10.2 months. In limited-stage SCLC treated with PE plus locoregional RT, median OS reaches 20-25 months with 5-year survival of 12-20%. Laryngeal SCNEC treated with SCLC-paradigm chemotherapy achieves similar response rates (60-70%) and median OS of 18-22 months for Stage III (analogous to limited-stage SCLC), substantially exceeding outcomes with HNSCC-directed therapy alone.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin and etoposide are both generic chemotherapy agents available at any oncology infusion center worldwide. The PE regimen requires standard oncology infrastructure: IV administration, CBC monitoring, and renal function testing. Both agents have been off-patent for decades and are among the lowest-cost chemotherapy options available. Insurance coverage is universal for SCLC-paradigm PE in neuroendocrine carcinoma. ECOG 0 status and age 45 allow full-dose cisplatin administration without dose reduction.

safety_profile.rationale: PE chemotherapy carries manageable but significant toxicity. Cisplatin causes nephrotoxicity (requiring aggressive hydration), ototoxicity (grade 2-3 in 20-30%), and peripheral neuropathy. Etoposide causes myelosuppression (grade 3-4 neutropenia in 60-70%), alopecia, and mucositis. ECOG 0 performance status and age 45 allow tolerance of full-dose PE with G-CSF support. Cumulative cisplatin toxicity limits to approximately 6 cycles. Antiemetic prophylaxis (5-HT3 antagonist plus NK1 antagonist) is mandatory.

biomarker_match.rationale: The molecular profile of this tumor â€” synaptophysin+, chromogranin+, Ki-67 90%, RB1 loss, TP53 mutated â€” is diagnostic of pulmonary-type small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma, the precise indication for PE chemotherapy. TTF-1 negativity confirms laryngeal primary (not lung metastasis) but does not alter treatment. RB1 loss is characteristic of SCLC and drives sensitivity to etoposide (topoisomerase II inhibitor), making this the most molecularly matched treatment for this tumor's biology.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms covalent platinum-DNA adducts causing intrastrand and interstrand crosslinks that block DNA replication and transcription, triggering apoptosis through p53-independent mitochondrial pathways â€” particularly relevant in TP53-mutated SCNEC. Etoposide is a topoisomerase II inhibitor that stabilizes the topoisomerase II-DNA complex, preventing DNA strand re-ligation and causing double-strand breaks. RB1 loss in SCNEC creates particular sensitivity to etoposide by deregulating E2F transcription factors and accelerating tumor cells through S-phase where topoisomerase II activity is highest. The synergistic combination achieves high response rates (60-70%) characteristic of SCLC.

key_evidence.study_name: Roth et al. PE vs Alternating Chemotherapy for Extensive SCLC

treatments.availability: Standard of care for SCLC and extrapulmonary SCNEC â€” available at all oncology centers; cisplatin and etoposide are generic agents; NCCN Category 1 recommendation

treatments.name: Carboplatin + Etoposide (CE â€” Cisplatin-Alternative Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Carboplatin-etoposide has equivalent efficacy to cisplatin-etoposide in multiple SCLC randomized trials including Okamoto et al. JNCI 2007 (N=220, limited-stage SCLC, OS non-inferior). Sundstrom et al. JCO 2002 (N=436) demonstrated carboplatin/etoposide non-inferior to cisplatin/etoposide in extensive SCLC. NCCN 2025 lists CE as Category 1 equivalent to PE. The SCLC-paradigm evidence base directly applies to laryngeal SCNEC per NCCN SCNEC guidelines.

survival_benefit.rationale: Okamoto et al. JNCI 2007 (N=220 limited-stage SCLC) showed carboplatin/etoposide achieved median OS of 20.8 months vs 22.4 months for cisplatin/etoposide (non-inferior, HR 1.04). In extensive-stage SCLC, Sundstrom et al. JCO 2002 showed OS 8.9 vs 9.9 months (p=0.6, non-inferior). Carboplatin AUC 5-6 achieves equivalent antitumor activity to cisplatin 75-80 mg/m2 in SCLC/SCNEC without the nephrotoxicity burden, allowing full-dose treatment in patients with borderline renal function.

accessibility.rationale: Carboplatin is a generic, off-patent agent available at all oncology centers at lower cost than cisplatin. Carboplatin does not require the aggressive hydration protocol needed for cisplatin, simplifying outpatient administration. The Calvert formula dose calculation (based on GFR) is standard practice. Carboplatin is preferred over cisplatin at many institutions due to its superior tolerability profile while maintaining equivalent efficacy in SCLC. Insurance coverage is universal for carboplatin in neuroendocrine carcinoma.

safety_profile.rationale: Carboplatin is substantially more nephroprotective and ototoxicity-sparing than cisplatin. The primary toxicity of carboplatin is hematologic: grade 3-4 thrombocytopenia (25-35%) and neutropenia (60-70%). Carboplatin causes less nausea/vomiting (grade 2-3 in 30-40% vs 60-70% with cisplatin), no significant nephrotoxicity, and minimal ototoxicity. These advantages make CE preferable in patients with pre-existing renal impairment, hearing loss, or neuropathy, and generally better tolerated for elderly or performance-compromised patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: For this ECOG 0, age 45 patient with 35 pack-year smoking history and T3N1M0 laryngeal SCNEC, carboplatin-etoposide is equally appropriate to cisplatin-etoposide given equivalent SCLC efficacy data. The synaptophysin+/chromogranin+/RB1 loss/Ki-67 90% molecular profile confirms SCNEC that is exquisitely sensitive to platinum-etoposide, with RB1 loss enhancing etoposide sensitivity. This patient can tolerate full-dose cisplatin (ECOG 0, age 45), making CE a preferred alternative only if cisplatin toxicity emerges during treatment.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Carboplatin forms the same type of platinum-DNA intrastrand and interstrand crosslinks as cisplatin but with slower aquation kinetics, resulting in equivalent cytotoxicity but different toxicity profiles (primarily hematologic vs nephrotoxic/neurotoxic for cisplatin). The carboplatin-DNA adducts inhibit DNA replication and transcription, triggering apoptosis through p53-independent pathways in TP53-mutated SCNEC. Etoposide's mechanism (topoisomerase II inhibition) is synergistic with platinum-DNA crosslinks, as cells with unrepaired platinum adducts are particularly vulnerable to topoisomerase II-mediated double-strand breaks during DNA replication.

key_evidence.study_name: Okamoto H et al. Phase 3 Carboplatin/Etoposide vs Cisplatin/Etoposide in Limited-Stage SCLC
key_evidence.journal: Journal of the National Cancer Institute

treatments.availability: Standard of care for SCLC and SCNEC â€” available at all oncology centers; carboplatin is a generic agent; dosing by Calvert formula (AUC 5-6); NCCN Category 1 recommendation

treatments.name: Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation (PCI, 25 Gy/10 fr, After Chemotherapy Response)

evidence_level.rationale: Auperin et al. NEJM 1999 meta-analysis (N=987 limited-stage and extensive SCLC in complete remission) established PCI as standard of care, showing significant improvement in 3-year OS from 15.3% to 20.7% (HR 0.84, p=0.01) and reduction in cumulative 3-year brain metastasis incidence from 58.6% to 33.3%. Le Pechoux et al. Lancet Oncol 2009 defined optimal PCI dose at 25 Gy in 10 fractions. Extrapulmonary SCNEC has equal or greater propensity for CNS metastasis, making PCI evidence directly applicable.

survival_benefit.rationale: Auperin et al. NEJM 1999 meta-analysis (N=987 SCLC) demonstrated PCI improved 3-year OS from 15.3% to 20.7% (HR 0.84, p=0.01) and reduced brain metastasis risk by 54% (58.6% to 33.3%, p<0.001). Le Pechoux et al. Lancet Oncol 2009 confirmed 25 Gy/10 fr as effective as 36 Gy with less neurotoxicity. For laryngeal SCNEC with high Ki-67 (90%) and systemic biology, brain metastasis rate without PCI is estimated at 40-60% at 3 years, making PCI an OS-improving intervention in patients achieving response to PE.

accessibility.rationale: Prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions is a simple, short whole-brain radiation course available at any radiation oncology center with a linear accelerator. No specialized equipment is required. The 2-week treatment course is administered after documented response to initial chemotherapy (typically after 4-6 cycles). Insurance coverage is universal for PCI in SCLC/SCNEC following systemic response, and the treatment is straightforward to deliver with standard simulation and planning techniques.

safety_profile.rationale: PCI at 25 Gy/10 fractions causes acute fatigue, alopecia, and mild headache universally. Late neurocognitive effects are the primary long-term concern: memory impairment (grade 2-3 in 20-30%), attention and executive function decline, and rarely radiation leukoencephalopathy. Hippocampal avoidance PCI (HA-PCI) reduces neurocognitive decline by 26% (RTOG 0933) while maintaining prophylactic efficacy. At age 45, neurocognitive preservation is particularly important, supporting use of HA-PCI technique when feasible.

biomarker_match.rationale: Ki-67 90% and small cell biology with RB1 loss in this laryngeal SCNEC confer high risk of subclinical CNS micrometastases, analogous to SCLC. PCI is indicated in patients achieving complete or partial response to systemic chemotherapy (PE or CE). The SCLC-paradigm management of laryngeal SCNEC explicitly includes PCI for responding patients per NCCN 2025 guidelines for high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma. Response assessment by PET-CT and brain MRI before PCI is required to exclude overt brain metastases.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Prophylactic cranial irradiation delivers whole-brain radiation to sterilize subclinical micrometastatic SCNEC deposits in the brain parenchyma that are not detectable on MRI at the time of treatment. SCNEC cells with Ki-67 90% proliferation and SCLC biology have high propensity for hematogenous CNS seeding. PCI at 25 Gy/10 fractions delivers tumoricidal doses to micrometastatic foci while maintaining acceptable neurotoxicity through use of hippocampal-avoidance IMRT technique. The blood-brain barrier penetration of systemic chemotherapy is insufficient to reliably eliminate CNS micrometastases, making dedicated brain irradiation necessary.

key_evidence.study_name: Auperin A et al. PCI Meta-Analysis â€” Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation for SCLC in Complete Remission

biomarker_requirements: Complete or partial response to initial PE/CE chemotherapy
Brain MRI negative for overt metastases at PCI initiation
treatments.availability: Standard of care for responding SCLC/SCNEC â€” available at all radiation oncology centers; hippocampal-avoidance PCI (HA-PCI) preferred for younger patients to preserve neurocognition; NCCN Category 1 for responding patients

treatments.name: Locoregional Radiation Therapy Concurrent with Chemotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Turrisi et al. NEJM 1999 (Intergroup 0096) established twice-daily concurrent CRT as superior to once-daily RT for limited-stage SCLC, with subsequent confirmation that early concurrent CRT improves OS in limited-stage disease. Jeremic et al. JCO 1997 demonstrated concurrent CRT superiority over sequential therapy. NCCN 2025 and ESMO guidelines mandate locoregional RT for limited-stage/Stage III SCNEC treated with curative intent, with concurrent platinum-etoposide providing radiosensitization.

survival_benefit.rationale: Turrisi et al. NEJM 1999 (N=417 limited-stage SCLC) demonstrated concurrent twice-daily RT improved 5-year OS from 16% to 26% vs once-daily RT (HR 0.82, p=0.04). Warde et al. JCO 1992 meta-analysis (N=2,140) showed combined modality therapy improves 3-year OS by 5.4% and local control by 25% in limited-stage SCLC. For laryngeal SCNEC Stage III, locoregional RT to the primary and involved nodes concurrent with PE is expected to achieve 5-year OS of 20-25%, substantially better than chemotherapy alone.

accessibility.rationale: Thoracic/locoregional radiation therapy concurrent with chemotherapy is available at all radiation oncology departments with linear accelerator capability. IMRT for laryngeal and cervical nodal irradiation is a standard technique at any radiation oncology facility. Modern IMRT reduces dose to the spinal cord, contralateral salivary glands, and pharyngeal constrictors compared to 3D-CRT. Scheduling requires coordination between medical oncology and radiation oncology departments but is universally available.

safety_profile.rationale: Concurrent CRT for laryngeal SCNEC involves radiation to the larynx, hypopharynx, and cervical/mediastinal nodes, causing significant acute toxicity including mucositis (grade 3-4 in 40-50%), dysphagia requiring feeding tube in 25-35% of patients, and esophagitis. Concurrent etoposide enhances mucositis risk. Late effects include xerostomia, voice changes, and radiation pneumonitis if mediastinal nodes are included. Esophageal stricture risk approximately 5-10% with modern IMRT. Overall grade 3-4 toxicity rate approximately 40-50%.

biomarker_match.rationale: T3N1M0 Stage III laryngeal SCNEC with locoregional lymph node involvement represents the precise indication for concurrent CRT per SCLC-paradigm management. The locoregional disease without distant metastasis makes consolidative RT to the primary tumor and involved nodes oncologically rational. Concurrent RT is given during or immediately after initial chemotherapy cycles (early concurrent approach) per Turrisi paradigm. The neuroendocrine biology of SCNEC (analogous to limited-stage SCLC) makes this the standard locoregional consolidation approach.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Ionizing radiation from linear accelerator photon beams induces DNA double-strand breaks in tumor cells, with SCNEC being highly radiosensitive due to its rapidly proliferating biology (Ki-67 90%). Concurrent etoposide acts as a radiosensitizer by arresting cells in G2/M phase (most radiosensitive phase) and inhibiting radiation damage repair via topoisomerase II inhibition. The laryngeal primary and cervical nodal disease are encompassed in the RT field (typically 45-50 Gy in 25-30 fractions), with concurrent chemotherapy addressing systemic micrometastatic disease and providing locoregional radiosensitization.

key_evidence.study_name: Turrisi AT et al. Intergroup 0096 â€” Twice-Daily versus Once-Daily Thoracic Radiotherapy in Limited SCLC

biomarker_requirements: Limited-stage / Stage III disease without distant metastases
ECOG PS 0-2 to tolerate concurrent CRT
Adequate pulmonary function if thoracic RT included
treatments.availability: Standard of care for limited-stage SCLC and Stage III SCNEC â€” available at all radiation oncology centers; IMRT technique universally available; early concurrent start preferred (cycle 1-2 of PE)

treatments.name: Atezolizumab + Carboplatin/Etoposide (IMpower133)

evidence_level.rationale: IMpower133 (Horn et al. NEJM 2018) is the pivotal Phase 3 trial establishing atezolizumab plus carboplatin/etoposide as the first-line standard for extensive-stage SCLC, leading to FDA approval in March 2019. This represents the highest level of evidence (Phase 3 randomized) for immunotherapy combined with chemotherapy in SCLC. Extrapolation to laryngeal SCNEC is supported by identical molecular biology (RB1 loss, TP53 mutation, high Ki-67) and SCLC-paradigm management guidelines for extrapulmonary SCNEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: IMpower133 (Horn et al. NEJM 2018, N=403) demonstrated atezolizumab plus CE improved OS from 10.3 to 12.3 months (HR 0.70, 95% CI 0.54-0.91, p=0.007) and PFS from 4.3 to 5.2 months (HR 0.77, p=0.02). The 12-month OS rate improved from 33.3% to 43.1%. The survival benefit is modest in absolute terms but statistically significant and clinically meaningful for SCLC, a disease with historically rapid progression. Updated 2-year follow-up confirmed OS benefit maintained at 12.9 vs 11.0 months.

accessibility.rationale: Atezolizumab (TECENTRIQ) is FDA-approved for extensive-stage SCLC in combination with carboplatin/etoposide and is available at all oncology centers. For laryngeal SCNEC, this represents an off-label indication that requires institutional tumor board approval and insurance prior authorization citing SCLC-paradigm management of SCNEC. PD-L1 testing is not required for SCLC atezolizumab approval; the benefit was observed regardless of PD-L1 status in IMpower133.

safety_profile.rationale: Adding atezolizumab to carboplatin/etoposide in IMpower133 did not significantly increase grade 3-4 adverse events compared to CE alone (56.6% vs 56.1%). Immune-related adverse events occurred in 39.9% of patients in the atezolizumab arm, with grade 3-4 irAEs in 7.2%. Hepatitis, hypothyroidism, and pneumonitis are the most common irAEs requiring corticosteroid management. The manageable toxicity profile without significant additional hematologic toxicity makes this combination acceptable for ECOG 0 patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: Atezolizumab benefit in IMpower133 was observed regardless of PD-L1 expression status â€” neither PD-L1 expression nor TMB predicted atezolizumab benefit in SCLC. This patient's laryngeal SCNEC with RB1 loss and TP53 mutation is typical of SCLC biology where PD-1/PD-L1 pathway expression is variable. The moderate biomarker match score reflects the biomarker-unselected nature of immunotherapy benefit in SCLC and the uncertainty of extrapolating to laryngeal SCNEC without extrapulmonary SCNEC-specific data.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Atezolizumab is an engineered IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting PD-L1 that blocks its interaction with both PD-1 and B7-1 on T cells, restoring anti-tumor immune responses. Unlike pembrolizumab and nivolumab (PD-1 inhibitors), atezolizumab's PD-L1 targeting preserves the PD-L2/PD-1 interaction, potentially reducing autoimmune toxicity. When combined with carboplatin/etoposide, the immunogenic cell death induced by chemotherapy (release of tumor antigens and danger signals) enhances the anti-tumor T cell responses potentiated by PD-L1 blockade, creating an additive or synergistic immunostimulatory effect.

key_evidence.study_name: Horn L et al. IMpower133 â€” Atezolizumab plus Chemotherapy vs Chemotherapy for Extensive SCLC

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 testing not required for SCLC approval (biomarker-unselected)
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for extensive-stage SCLC (March 2019); off-label for Stage III laryngeal SCNEC requires tumor board approval; available at all oncology centers; NCCN Category 1 for extensive SCLC; extrapolated to SCNEC per SCLC-paradigm guidelines

treatments.name: Durvalumab + Platinum/Etoposide (CASPIAN)

evidence_level.rationale: CASPIAN (Paz-Ares et al. Lancet 2019) is a Phase 3 randomized trial (N=537) demonstrating durvalumab plus platinum/etoposide improved OS vs chemotherapy alone in extensive-stage SCLC, leading to FDA approval. Updated data (Paz-Ares et al. Lancet Oncol 2021) confirmed sustained OS benefit. Together with IMpower133, CASPIAN provides two Phase 3 datasets supporting PD-L1 inhibition plus chemotherapy as the first-line standard for SCLC/SCNEC. The CASPIAN data directly supports this treatment paradigm by SCLC-to-SCNEC extrapolation.

survival_benefit.rationale: CASPIAN (Paz-Ares et al. Lancet 2019, N=537) demonstrated durvalumab plus PE improved OS from 10.3 to 13.0 months (HR 0.73, 95% CI 0.59-0.91, p=0.004) vs PE alone. 18-month OS was 34.0% vs 25.0% in the durvalumab arm. Updated 3-year data (Paz-Ares Lancet Oncol 2021) showed 3-year OS of 17.6% vs 5.8% with chemotherapy alone, indicating a meaningful tail of long-term survivors. PFS improved from 5.1 to 5.1 months (HR 0.78, p=0.0047) with durvalumab.

accessibility.rationale: Durvalumab (IMFINZI) is FDA-approved for extensive-stage SCLC in combination with etoposide plus either carboplatin or cisplatin. For laryngeal SCNEC, extrapolated use follows institutional SCLC-paradigm protocols with tumor board endorsement. Durvalumab 1500 mg Q4W is administered after an initial phase of chemotherapy priming. Coverage for off-label SCNEC use requires documentation of SCLC-like histology and molecular profile, which this patient clearly meets with synaptophysin+/chromogranin+/RB1 loss.

safety_profile.rationale: Durvalumab plus chemotherapy in CASPIAN showed grade 3-4 adverse events in 62.3% of patients (durvalumab arm) vs 62.8% (chemotherapy alone), indicating no significant immunotherapy-related toxicity increase. Immune-related adverse events occurred in 20.4% of durvalumab patients, with grade 3-4 in 3.3%. Hypothyroidism (7.4%), rash (5.0%), and hepatitis (2%) are the most common irAEs. The comparable toxicity profile to chemotherapy alone makes durvalumab a well-tolerated addition with acceptable risk-benefit ratio.

biomarker_match.rationale: Similar to atezolizumab in IMpower133, the CASPIAN trial did not identify a predictive biomarker for durvalumab benefit in SCLC â€” response was observed regardless of PD-L1 expression. For this laryngeal SCNEC patient, the SCLC-like molecular biology (RB1 loss, TP53 mutation, high Ki-67, neuroendocrine markers) is the primary rationale for SCLC-paradigm immunotherapy. The biomarker-unselected nature of durvalumab benefit in SCLC makes the match moderate for the specific molecular profile described.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Durvalumab is a human IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting PD-L1, blocking the PD-L1/PD-1 interaction to restore T cell anti-tumor activity. Like atezolizumab, durvalumab targets PD-L1 rather than PD-1, potentially offering advantages in preserving the PD-L2 pathway. Durvalumab's IgG1 framework enables antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) against PD-L1-expressing cells, providing an additional mechanism beyond checkpoint blockade. The combination with platinum/etoposide exploits chemotherapy-induced immunogenic cell death to enhance the anti-tumor immune response reinvigorated by PD-L1 blockade.

key_evidence.study_name: Paz-Ares L et al. CASPIAN â€” Durvalumab with or without Tremelimumab plus Platinum/Etoposide for Extensive SCLC

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 testing not required for SCLC indication
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for extensive-stage SCLC (March 2020); extrapolated to laryngeal SCNEC per SCLC-paradigm guidelines; available at all oncology centers; requires tumor board endorsement for laryngeal SCNEC application

treatments.name: Lurbinectedin (2nd-Line SCLC-Paradigm)

evidence_level.rationale: Lurbinectedin (ZEPZELCA) received FDA accelerated approval in June 2020 for adults with metastatic SCLC with disease progression on or after platinum-based chemotherapy, based on the PM1183-B-005-13 basket trial (Trigo et al. Lancet Oncol 2020, N=105 SCLC). This represents accelerated approval from a Phase 2 single-arm study; confirmatory Phase 3 trial (ATLANTIS) showed non-significant improvement vs topotecan. Extrapolation to laryngeal SCNEC is based on identical SCLC biology and NCCN endorsement for extrapulmonary SCNEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: Trigo et al. Lancet Oncol 2020 (N=105 SCLC) demonstrated lurbinectedin achieved ORR of 35.2%, median DOR of 5.3 months, and median OS of 9.3 months in platinum-sensitive and platinum-resistant R/R SCLC. In platinum-sensitive patients (chemotherapy-free interval >90 days), ORR was 45% with median OS of 11.9 months, comparing favorably to second-line topotecan (ORR 8-24%, OS 5-6 months). Lurbinectedin represents a meaningful treatment option after PE/CE failure in laryngeal SCNEC.

accessibility.rationale: Lurbinectedin is FDA-approved for metastatic SCLC and is commercially available in the United States. For laryngeal SCNEC, the same SCLC-paradigm rationale supports off-label use after PE/CE failure. IV administration every 3 weeks at 3.2 mg/m2 is a manageable outpatient infusion. Drug access programs and patient assistance programs are available through the manufacturer (Jazz Pharmaceuticals). Insurance coverage for off-label SCNEC use citing SCLC approval requires institutional prior authorization support.

safety_profile.rationale: Lurbinectedin has a relatively manageable toxicity profile. Hematologic toxicity is the primary concern: grade 3-4 neutropenia (46.5%), thrombocytopenia (7.5%), and anemia (6.8%). Febrile neutropenia occurred in 5.5% and G-CSF support is recommended. Non-hematologic toxicities include fatigue (33%), nausea (32%), decreased appetite (22%), and liver enzyme elevation (10-15%). No nephrotoxicity or ototoxicity, unlike cisplatin. The Q3W dosing schedule and absence of non-hematologic severe toxicities improve quality of life compared to topotecan.

biomarker_match.rationale: SCNEC with SCLC-like molecular profile (RB1 loss, TP53 mutation, synaptophysin/chromogranin positive, Ki-67 90%) is the precise indication for SCLC-paradigm second-line therapy with lurbinectedin. Transcription addiction and RNA polymerase II dependence characteristic of SCLC biology â€” where lurbinectedin exerts its primary mechanism â€” are expected features of high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma with this molecular profile. NCCN 2025 lists lurbinectedin as a Category 2A preferred second-line option for SCLC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lurbinectedin is a synthetic tetrahydroisoquinoline alkaloid (PM00489) that covalently binds to the minor groove of DNA at specific sequences (CG-rich), forming DNA adducts that inhibit RNA polymerase II-dependent transcription. SCNEC cells with high transcription addiction (Ki-67 90%, SCLC phenotype) are particularly dependent on RNA Pol II for rapid proliferation, making them exquisitely sensitive to transcription inhibition. Lurbinectedin also blocks active transcription-associated DNA repair, trapping topoisomerase I cleavage complexes and inducing double-strand breaks in actively transcribed regions of the genome.

key_evidence.study_name: Trigo J et al. Lurbinectedin as Second-Line Treatment for Patients with Small-Cell Lung Cancer

biomarker_requirements: SCNEC/SCLC histology with prior platinum-based chemotherapy
RB1 loss consistent with SCLC biology
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for metastatic SCLC after platinum failure; extrapolated to laryngeal SCNEC; available at all oncology centers; patient assistance programs available through manufacturer

treatments.name: Tarlatamab (DLL3 Bispecific T-Cell Engager)

evidence_level.rationale: DeLLphi-300 trial (Ahn et al. NEJM 2023) led to FDA accelerated approval of tarlatamab for extensive-stage SCLC after platinum-based chemotherapy failure. DeLLphi-304 Phase 3 confirmatory trial is ongoing (NCT05740982). Evidence is from Phase 2 single-arm trial in SCLC, with DLL3 expression confirmed in 85-95% of SCNEC tumors by IHC. Extrapolation to laryngeal SCNEC is supported by identical DLL3 expression patterns and SCLC-like biology, but formal approval is limited to pulmonary SCLC.

survival_benefit.rationale: Ahn et al. NEJM 2023 (DeLLphi-300, N=220 R/R SCLC) reported ORR of 40%, median DOR of 9.7 months, and median OS of 14.3 months with tarlatamab 10 mg Q2W. The 1-year OS rate of 60% represents a meaningful improvement over historical controls with second-line topotecan (ORR 8-24%, median OS 5-6 months). For laryngeal SCNEC with high DLL3 expression (expected >85%), similar ORR and OS benefit is extrapolated from the DeLLphi-300 data.

accessibility.rationale: Tarlatamab (IMDELLTRA) received FDA approval in May 2024 for extensive-stage SCLC after platinum-based chemotherapy. For laryngeal SCNEC, off-label use requires institutional tumor board review and DLL3 IHC testing to confirm expression. Insurance coverage for off-label SCNEC use is emerging but not uniformly approved. Clinical trial enrollment in DeLLphi-304 or basket trials including extrapulmonary SCNEC is strongly preferred. Specialized oncology centers are initiating institutional protocols for tarlatamab in SCNEC.

safety_profile.rationale: Tarlatamab's primary safety concern is cytokine release syndrome (CRS), which occurs in approximately 51% of patients (grade 1-2 in 46%, grade 3 in 5%) and requires mandatory 24-hour observation after initial doses. Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) occurs in 10% (grade 3 in 1%). Pyrexia (75%), decreased appetite (30%), and fatigue (28%) are common non-immune adverse events. First-dose hospitalization for CRS monitoring is required. Despite these risks, there are no hematologic or renal toxicities, distinguishing tarlatamab from chemotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: DLL3 (delta-like ligand 3) is expressed in 85-95% of SCLC and SCNEC tumors, including essentially all cases with Ki-67 >50% and RB1 loss â€” precisely the molecular profile of this laryngeal SCNEC. DLL3 is a Notch ligand aberrantly expressed on the surface of neuroendocrine carcinoma cells and absent from normal adult tissues, providing exceptional tumor specificity for tarlatamab. Ki-67 90% and high-grade SCNEC biology are strongly correlated with DLL3 expression, making this the highest biomarker match of any targeted agent for this patient.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tarlatamab is a bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) that simultaneously binds DLL3 on tumor cell surfaces and CD3 epsilon on T lymphocytes, physically crosslinking cytotoxic T cells with SCNEC tumor cells regardless of T cell antigen specificity or TCR repertoire. This proximity-forced interaction triggers T cell activation, leading to directed release of granzymes and perforin that cause tumor cell lysis. Unlike conventional antibodies requiring preexisting antitumor immunity, tarlatamab creates de novo immune synapse formation at the tumor cell surface, achieving tumor killing independent of PD-L1 status or prior T cell priming.

key_evidence.study_name: Ahn MJ et al. DeLLphi-300 â€” Tarlatamab for Previously Treated Small Cell Lung Cancer

biomarker_requirements: DLL3 expression by IHC â‰¥1% (>85% of SCNEC)
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for extensive-stage SCLC (May 2024); off-label for laryngeal SCNEC requires tumor board review and DLL3 IHC; clinical trial enrollment preferred; DLL3 IHC testing available at major pathology laboratories

treatments.name: Topotecan (Second-Line SCLC-Paradigm)

evidence_level.rationale: Topotecan is FDA-approved for second-line SCLC based on von Pawel et al. JCO 1999 (Phase 3, N=211, topotecan vs CAV) and Eckardt et al. JCO 2007 (oral topotecan vs IV in sensitive SCLC). NCCN SCLC guidelines list topotecan as a Category 1 preferred second-line agent. Topotecan's approval and guideline endorsement for SCLC directly supports its use in laryngeal SCNEC per SCLC-paradigm management. Evidence is robust for platinum-sensitive and platinum-resistant settings.

survival_benefit.rationale: Von Pawel et al. JCO 1999 (N=211) showed topotecan vs CAV in sensitive SCLC achieved ORR of 24% vs 18% and median OS of 25.0 vs 24.7 weeks (non-inferior, with symptom benefit advantage). In platinum-resistant SCLC, topotecan ORR is 8-16% with median OS of 4-6 months. Topotecan's survival benefit is modest but represents the historical benchmark for second-line SCLC against which newer agents are compared. For laryngeal SCNEC after PE failure, median OS of 5-6 months is extrapolated.

accessibility.rationale: Topotecan is FDA-approved for SCLC and is available as both IV and oral formulations at all oncology centers. The oral formulation (2.3 mg/m2 days 1-5, Q21d) allows outpatient administration without IV access requirement. Topotecan is a generic agent with low cost compared to newer second-line options. Insurance coverage for SCLC-paradigm use in laryngeal SCNEC is broadly available with appropriate documentation. The oral formulation is particularly practical for patients preferring outpatient management.

safety_profile.rationale: Topotecan causes significant hematologic toxicity: grade 3-4 neutropenia (70-80%), thrombocytopenia (35-40%), and anemia (30-40%), often requiring dose reductions, growth factor support, and transfusions. Febrile neutropenia occurs in 15-20% despite G-CSF prophylaxis. Non-hematologic toxicities include nausea, diarrhea, and fatigue. The high myelosuppression rate limits topotecan's practical utility in heavily pre-treated patients and makes it less tolerable than lurbinectedin or tarlatamab in the second-line setting.

biomarker_match.rationale: Topoisomerase I, the target of topotecan, is highly expressed in SCLC and SCNEC tumors, particularly those with high Ki-67 and RB1 loss. Topotecan's mechanism specifically targets rapidly proliferating cells in S-phase (when topoisomerase I activity is highest), matching the Ki-67 90% proliferation rate of this laryngeal SCNEC. SCLC-paradigm guidelines endorse topotecan for SCNEC, and the RB1-loss/TP53-mutation molecular profile is consistent with topoisomerase I sensitivity patterns seen in SCLC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Topotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin analog that inhibits topoisomerase I by stabilizing the covalent topoisomerase I-DNA cleavage complex, preventing re-ligation of single-strand DNA breaks. During DNA replication and transcription, the trapped topoisomerase I complex converts to lethal double-strand breaks through collisions with replication forks. Rapidly proliferating SCNEC cells with Ki-67 90% generate extensive topoisomerase I-DNA complexes during rapid replication, making them selectively vulnerable to topotecan-induced replication fork collapse and double-strand break formation.

key_evidence.study_name: von Pawel J et al. Phase 3 Topotecan vs CAV in Second-Line Sensitive SCLC

biomarker_requirements: Topoisomerase I expression (universal in SCNEC, not routinely tested)
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for second-line SCLC; available as IV and oral formulations at all oncology centers; generic agent; extrapolated to laryngeal SCNEC per SCLC-paradigm guidelines

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab (TMB-High, KEYNOTE-158 Tumor-Agnostic)

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-158 (Marabelle et al. JCO 2020) supports FDA approval of pembrolizumab for TMB-high (â‰¥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors regardless of histology, providing a tumor-agnostic pathway for laryngeal SCNEC. KEYNOTE-028 (phase 1b, SCLC cohort) and KEYNOTE-158 SCLC cohort showed ORR of 33% in PD-L1+ SCLC. For TMB-high SCNEC, the tumor-agnostic FDA approval provides the regulatory basis. TP53 mutation in SCNEC may elevate TMB, but this must be confirmed by comprehensive genomic profiling.

survival_benefit.rationale: Marabelle et al. JCO 2020 (KEYNOTE-158, N=1,066 across 10 tumor types) demonstrated pembrolizumab in TMB-high tumors achieved ORR of 29% (vs 6% TMB-low) with median DOR not reached (>47% lasting 12+ months). In the SCLC cohort of KEYNOTE-158, ORR was 17% overall and 33% in PD-L1+ patients. Median OS in the TMB-high SCLC subgroup was approximately 8-10 months with durable responses. For laryngeal SCNEC with confirmed TMB-high status, similar ORR and durable response rates are expected.

accessibility.rationale: Pembrolizumab is FDA-approved for TMB-high solid tumors (tumor-agnostic, June 2020) requiring TMB â‰¥10 mut/Mb on an FDA-approved assay (Foundation Medicine FoundationOne CDx). TMB testing is broadly available at major pathology laboratories and through commercial genomic profiling. Insurance coverage for pembrolizumab in TMB-high solid tumors including SCNEC is available with appropriate testing documentation. The tumor-agnostic approval provides clear regulatory support for pembrolizumab in this setting without requiring SCNEC-specific trial data.

safety_profile.rationale: Pembrolizumab monotherapy has a well-characterized, manageable toxicity profile with immune-related adverse events (irAEs) as the primary concern. Grade 3-4 irAEs occur in approximately 10-15% of patients. Common irAEs include hypothyroidism (10-15%), dermatitis (10-15%), pneumonitis (3-5%), and colitis (2-3%). The absence of hematologic toxicity (unlike chemotherapy) and the steroid-responsive nature of irAEs make pembrolizumab well-tolerated. The favorable safety profile is particularly advantageous following cumulative chemotherapy toxicity in the relapsed SCNEC setting.

biomarker_match.rationale: TMB â‰¥10 mut/Mb is the required biomarker for tumor-agnostic pembrolizumab approval. TP53 mutation in SCNEC may contribute to TMB elevation, but SCLC/SCNEC typically has TMB in the 5-10 mut/Mb range â€” near the threshold â€” so TMB-high status (â‰¥10 mut/Mb) must be confirmed by comprehensive genomic profiling before this treatment is indicated. Smoking history (35 pack-years) may increase TMB through tobacco-associated mutagenic signature. Comprehensive genomic profiling (FoundationOne CDx) is required before pembrolizumab can be recommended.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab blocks PD-1 on cytotoxic T lymphocytes, releasing the inhibitory signal imposed by tumor-expressed PD-L1 and PD-L2. In TMB-high tumors, abundant somatic mutations generate neoantigens â€” mutant peptides presented on MHC class I â€” that prime cytotoxic T cells to recognize tumor cells as non-self. PD-1 blockade reinvigorates these neoantigen-specific T cells that have become exhausted in the tumor microenvironment, enabling sustained anti-tumor cytotoxicity. The higher mutational burden correlates with greater neoantigen diversity and improved pembrolizumab response.

key_evidence.study_name: Marabelle A et al. KEYNOTE-158 â€” Efficacy of Pembrolizumab in Patients with TMB-High Solid Tumors

biomarker_requirements: TMB â‰¥10 mutations/megabase on FDA-approved assay (FoundationOne CDx)
Prior platinum-based chemotherapy in 2nd line or beyond
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for TMB-high solid tumors (tumor-agnostic, 2020); requires FoundationOne CDx or equivalent TMB testing; available at all oncology centers; covered by most major insurers with TMB documentation

treatments.name: AMG 757 (Tarlatamab Phase 1/2 â€” Historical DeLLphi-300 Reference)

evidence_level.rationale: AMG 757 (the investigational name for tarlatamab before approval) was studied in DeLLphi-301 Phase 1 dose-escalation trial (Lahiri et al. Cancer Cell 2022, N=73), establishing safety and dose selection for Phase 2. Phase 1 data showed ORR of 19% at the 10 mg dose and 23% at the 100 mg dose with manageable CRS. This Phase 1 evidence led to DeLLphi-300 Phase 2 and FDA approval. For laryngeal SCNEC, the DeLLphi-300 Phase 2 data (now the basis of FDA approval) represents the most clinically mature evidence.

survival_benefit.rationale: DeLLphi-301 Phase 1 (Lahiri et al. Cancer Cell 2022, N=73 R/R SCLC) demonstrated tarlatamab 10 mg ORR of 19%, DOR not reached at median follow-up, and median OS of 13.2 months. Early Phase 1 data with small sample sizes and selected patients limits survival benefit assessment. The subsequent Phase 2 (DeLLphi-300) with ORR 40% and OS 14.3 months (N=220) represents the mature evidence that supersedes Phase 1 data. Included separately to acknowledge the investigational history of DLL3 bispecific T-cell engager development.

accessibility.rationale: AMG 757 as an investigational drug is now superseded by FDA-approved tarlatamab. The Phase 1 dose-escalation trials are closed. Access to DLL3 BiTE therapy is now through commercial tarlatamab (FDA-approved) or active Phase 3 trials (DeLLphi-304). This entry is retained to acknowledge the developmental history and the Phase 1 evidence base that informs optimal dosing (10 mg Q2W selected for Phase 2 based on Phase 1 safety/efficacy). Patients should be directed to tarlatamab commercial use or DeLLphi-304 enrollment.

safety_profile.rationale: Phase 1 DeLLphi-301 demonstrated manageable CRS profile with 74% CRS rate (mostly grade 1-2) that established the mandatory 24-hour first-dose monitoring requirement carried into commercial tarlatamab. ICANS occurred in 8% with grade 3 in 1%. The Phase 1 safety findings directly informed the monitoring requirements for approved tarlatamab, demonstrating that step-up dosing and premedication strategies can mitigate severe CRS. Overall safety profile acceptable and consistent with the BiTE mechanism class.

biomarker_match.rationale: DLL3 expression confirmed in >85% of SCLC/SCNEC tumor samples in DeLLphi-301 correlates directly with this patient's laryngeal SCNEC molecular profile. The Ki-67 90%, RB1 loss, and SCLC-like neuroendocrine markers are strongly associated with DLL3 surface expression, making DLL3-targeted BiTE therapy the most molecularly rational targeted approach for this tumor type. This historical entry reinforces the scientific basis for preferring DLL3-directed therapy (tarlatamab) over non-targeted immunotherapy in DLL3-expressing SCNEC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: AMG 757 (tarlatamab) Phase 1 established the bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) mechanism and dose-response relationship for DLL3 targeting in SCLC. The bispecific antibody simultaneously binds DLL3 on tumor cells and CD3 epsilon on T cells, forming an immunological synapse that triggers T cell-mediated tumor lysis independent of antigen presentation machinery. Phase 1 pharmacodynamic data demonstrated dose-dependent T cell expansion, serum cytokine elevation (IL-6, IL-10 correlating with CRS), and tumor DLL3 receptor occupancy at clinically active doses. This mechanistic insight led to the 10 mg Q2W dose selection for Phase 2.

key_evidence.study_name: Lahiri M et al. DeLLphi-301 Phase 1 â€” AMG 757 (Tarlatamab) in Relapsed/Refractory SCLC

biomarker_requirements: DLL3 expression by IHC (enrolled all-comers in Phase 1; DLL3 expression retrospectively confirmed >85%)
treatments.availability: Phase 1 trials closed; superseded by FDA-approved tarlatamab (IMDELLTRA); DeLLphi-304 Phase 3 ongoing (NCT05740982); commercial tarlatamab is preferred access pathway for eligible patients

treatments.name: Octreotide (Somatostatin Analogue â€” SSTR-Positive Component)

evidence_level.rationale: PROMID trial (Rinke et al. JCO 2009) and CLARINET trial (Caplin et al. NEJM 2014) established somatostatin analogues (SSAs) for low- and intermediate-grade neuroendocrine tumors but not high-grade SCNEC. Ki-67 90% SCNEC is typically SSTR-negative or weakly positive, and SSA evidence is limited to grade 1-2 NETs. Small series report occasional SSA benefit in somatostatin receptor-positive SCNEC, but evidence is anecdotal. Included as an experimental option pending SSTR2 IHC/PET-CT Ga-DOTATATE imaging to confirm receptor expression.

survival_benefit.rationale: Rinke et al. JCO 2009 (PROMID, N=85 midgut NET grade 1) demonstrated octreotide LAR extended median TTP from 6.0 to 14.3 months (HR 0.34, p=0.000072) but only in low-grade SSTR-positive midgut NETs. This result does not apply to Ki-67 90% SCNEC, where SSTR expression is typically absent or minimal. In rare SSTR2-positive high-grade SCNEC cases, disease stabilization rather than response is the expected outcome. The survival benefit in laryngeal SCNEC with Ki-67 90% is highly uncertain and likely minimal.

accessibility.rationale: Octreotide LAR (Sandostatin LAR, monthly IM injection) and lanreotide (Somatuline, subcutaneous) are commercially available at all oncology centers. Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT for SSTR imaging is available at most academic medical centers. SSTR2 IHC can be performed on the existing tumor biopsy. Insurance coverage for SSAs in high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma requires documentation of positive SSTR expression; coverage for Ki-67 >20% tumors may be limited. This treatment is low-cost compared to targeted biologic agents.

safety_profile.rationale: Somatostatin analogues have an excellent safety profile. Common adverse effects include diarrhea/steatorrhea (30-40%), abdominal cramping (20-30%), cholelithiasis (20-30% long-term), and mild glucose dysregulation. These effects are manageable and rarely dose-limiting. The absence of hematologic toxicity, nephrotoxicity, or immune toxicity makes octreotide one of the safest systemic agents for neuroendocrine tumors. In the context of prior PE chemotherapy, the minimal overlapping toxicity is a significant practical advantage.

biomarker_match.rationale: Ki-67 90% SCNEC is characteristically SSTR2-negative or weakly positive by IHC, and Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT is expected to show low or absent uptake in high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma. This patient's molecular profile (Ki-67 90%, RB1 loss, TP53 mutation, chromogranin/synaptophysin positive) is characteristic of high-grade SCNEC that does not express functional somatostatin receptors. SSTR imaging must be performed before any SSA treatment; this option is conditionally included only if imaging reveals unexpected high SSTR2 expression.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Octreotide is a synthetic somatostatin analogue that binds somatostatin receptor subtype 2 (SSTR2), activating inhibitory G-protein coupled signaling that reduces cAMP, inhibits ion channel activity, and decreases intracellular calcium. In SSTR-expressing neuroendocrine tumors, octreotide reduces secretion of bioactive peptides (serotonin, substance P, VIP), inhibits growth hormone and IGF-1 signaling, reduces VEGF secretion, and directly inhibits tumor cell proliferation via SSTR2-mediated apoptosis induction and cell cycle arrest. Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) with Lu-177 DOTATATE represents an intensified SSTR-directed approach if Ga-DOTATATE uptake is documented.

key_evidence.study_name: Rinke A et al. PROMID â€” Octreotide LAR Placebo-Controlled Randomized Trial in Midgut NETs

biomarker_requirements: SSTR2 positive by IHC (Allred score â‰¥2) or Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT positive
Ki-67 <55% recommended for SSA benefit (high-grade SCNEC typically not eligible)
SSTR imaging mandatory before treatment initiation
treatments.availability: Commercially available octreotide LAR and lanreotide at all oncology centers; use in Ki-67 90% SCNEC requires positive SSTR imaging; insurance coverage conditional on SSTR expression documentation; Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT available at academic medical centers

treatments.name: Rovalpituzumab Tesirine (DLL3-ADC â€” Investigational Reference)

evidence_level.rationale: TRINITY trial (Morgensztern et al. JCO 2019, N=339 DLL3-high R/R SCLC) and TAHOE trial (Blackhall et al. Lancet Oncol 2021) established rovalpituzumab tesirine (Rova-T) activity but also its toxicity profile leading to clinical hold and withdrawal. Rova-T demonstrated DLL3 targeting feasibility in SCLC and SCNEC, providing proof-of-concept for DLL3-directed therapy. Although withdrawn from clinical development (2020), its data informs the DLL3-targeting strategy and supports tarlatamab development for this patient's laryngeal SCNEC.

survival_benefit.rationale: TRINITY trial (Morgensztern et al. JCO 2019, N=339) demonstrated Rova-T ORR of 18% in DLL3-high R/R SCLC with median PFS of 3.0 months and OS of 5.6 months. The TAHOE trial was halted early due to shorter OS in the Rova-T arm vs topotecan (6.1 vs 7.1 months, HR 1.46). While Rova-T showed target engagement, clinical benefit was insufficient and safety concerns (pleural effusion, edema, photosensitivity from SG3199 warhead) led to development termination. Included as historical/educational reference for DLL3 targeting.

accessibility.rationale: Rovalpituzumab tesirine was voluntarily withdrawn from clinical development by AbbVie in 2020 after disappointing Phase 3 results. It is not commercially available, not FDA-approved, and is no longer in active clinical trials. This treatment is included as an educational reference demonstrating DLL3-targeted ADC activity and supporting the mechanistic rationale for tarlatamab (a superior DLL3-targeting approach). Physicians should not pursue Rova-T as a clinical option; tarlatamab is the available DLL3-targeted therapy.

safety_profile.rationale: Rova-T's SG3199 (PBD dimer) warhead caused significant off-target toxicity including capillary leak syndrome, severe pleural effusion (grade 3-4 in 10-15%), peripheral edema (40-50%), severe photosensitivity reactions (30-40%), and thrombocytopenia. The narrow therapeutic window and predictable dose-limiting toxicities ultimately contributed to the decision to terminate development. Safety profile substantially inferior to tarlatamab, which achieves superior DLL3 targeting through bispecific T-cell engagement without cytotoxic warhead-related tissue toxicity. The severe capillary leak syndrome, pleural effusions, and photosensitivity reactions observed with rovalpituzumab tesirine in the TRINITY and TAHOE trials ultimately led to clinical hold and voluntary withdrawal. Toxicity profile was unacceptable with life-threatening pleural effusions in 10-15% and systemic photosensitivity in 30-40% of patients, leading to grade 4 events in a significant proportion and contributing to OS detriment in the TAHOE trial.

biomarker_match.rationale: DLL3 expression is present in >85% of SCNEC tumors with SCLC-like biology including Ki-67 90% and RB1 loss. This laryngeal SCNEC has the precise molecular profile (synaptophysin+/chromogranin+/RB1 loss/high Ki-67) associated with DLL3 overexpression in TRINITY trial eligibility criteria (DLL3 â‰¥75% by IHC). The DLL3-high expression provides the mechanistic rationale for DLL3-directed therapy broadly, informing the preferential use of tarlatamab as the currently available DLL3-targeting agent.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Rovalpituzumab tesirine is an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) composed of an anti-DLL3 antibody (SC16) linked to a highly potent DNA-alkylating pyrrolobenzodiazepine (PBD) dimer cytotoxin (SG3199). After binding DLL3 on SCNEC tumor cells, the ADC is internalized, the SG3199 warhead is released intracellularly, and the PBD dimer crosslinks DNA in a sequence-specific manner causing double-strand breaks and triggering apoptosis. A bystander killing effect allows SG3199 to also kill adjacent DLL3-negative tumor cells through diffusion, potentially overcoming tumor heterogeneity. Development was terminated due to unacceptable safety profile.

key_evidence.study_name: Morgensztern D et al. TRINITY â€” Rovalpituzumab Tesirine in Third-Line DLL3-High SCLC

biomarker_requirements: DLL3 expression â‰¥75% by IHC (for original TRINITY eligibility)
Not currently available â€” withdrawn from development
Reference only: supports tarlatamab as preferred DLL3-targeted option
notable_side_effects: Withdrawn from development due to unfavorable benefit-risk profile
treatments.availability: Not available â€” voluntarily withdrawn from clinical development by AbbVie in 2020 after Phase 3 results; no active clinical trials; historical reference only; tarlatamab preferred for DLL3-targeted therapy

clinical_trials.title: IMpower133 â€” Atezolizumab Combined with Carboplatin and Etoposide in Untreated Extensive-Stage SCLC
clinical_trials.status: Completed â€” FDA approval granted March 2019
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: No biomarker requirement; all-comers for SCLC/SCNEC

clinical_trials.title: CASPIAN â€” Durvalumab with or without Tremelimumab Plus Platinum/Etoposide for Extensive-Stage SCLC
clinical_trials.status: Completed â€” FDA approval granted March 2020
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: No biomarker requirement; all-comers SCLC/SCNEC histology

clinical_trials.title: DeLLphi-304 â€” Tarlatamab vs Investigator's Choice in Extensive-Stage SCLC After Platinum-Based Chemotherapy
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: SCLC/SCNEC histology; DLL3 IHC testing required; prior platinum-based chemotherapy

clinical_trials.title: Tarlatamab Plus Atezolizumab in Extensive-Stage SCLC and Extrapulmonary SCNEC (First-Line)
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: DLL3 expression by IHC; extrapulmonary SCNEC including laryngeal eligible

clinical_trials.title: Vorinostat Plus Pembrolizumab in Recurrent/Refractory Solid Tumors Including Neuroendocrine Carcinoma
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: Solid tumor with epigenetic biomarkers preferred; SCNEC eligible

clinical_trials.title: SCLC-Paradigm Immunotherapy in Extrapulmonary High-Grade Neuroendocrine Carcinomas â€” Basket Trial
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: High-grade NEC (Ki-67 >20%), extrapulmonary primary confirmed; SCNEC morphology

clinical_trials.title: Lu-177 DOTATATE (PRRT) in SSTR-Positive High-Grade Neuroendocrine Carcinomas
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: SSTR2 positive by Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT (SUVmax â‰¥ normal liver); Ki-67 any grade eligible

combination_strategies.name: Cisplatin/Etoposide + Atezolizumab (IMpower133-Derived)
combination_strategies.base_therapy: Cisplatin 75 mg/m2 D1 + Etoposide 100 mg/m2 D1-3, Q3W x 4 cycles
combination_strategies.combination_partner: Atezolizumab 1200 mg Q3W (concurrent with chemotherapy, then maintenance)
combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase 3 (IMpower133) with FDA approval for SCLC
combination_strategies.rationale: IMpower133 established that adding atezolizumab to carboplatin/etoposide improves OS in extensive-stage SCLC, and the same principle applies when using cisplatin instead of carboplatin for this ECOG 0, age 45 patient who can tolerate full-dose cisplatin. Immunogenic cell death induced by platinum-etoposide chemotherapy releases tumor antigens and danger signals that prime dendritic cells, enhancing the PD-L1 blockade effect of atezolizumab. Maintenance atezolizumab after 4 cycles continues immune surveillance, reducing relapse risk in responding patients with laryngeal SCNEC biology.

combination_strategies.name: PE Induction + Concurrent CRT + PCI Consolidation (SCLC-Limited Stage Protocol)
combination_strategies.base_therapy: Cisplatin + Etoposide x 4 cycles with early concurrent RT (cycle 1 or 2)
combination_strategies.combination_partner: PCI 25 Gy/10 fr after documented locoregional response, + maintenance durvalumab
combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase 3 (Turrisi Intergroup 0096 + CASPIAN) â€” sequenced combination
combination_strategies.rationale: This sequenced trimodality approach â€” cisplatin/etoposide with concurrent locoregional RT (early concurrent as per Intergroup 0096) followed by PCI for CNS prophylaxis â€” mirrors the limited-stage SCLC standard of care and is directly applicable to Stage III laryngeal SCNEC. Adding maintenance durvalumab after consolidative CRT is an investigational but biologically rational extension of the CASPIAN results, potentially extending the tail of long-term survivors through sustained immune checkpoint blockade following chemoradiotherapy-induced immune priming.

combination_strategies.name: Tarlatamab + Pembrolizumab (Dual Immune Checkpoint x BiTE)
combination_strategies.combination_partner: Pembrolizumab 200 mg Q6W (after initial CRS risk period for tarlatamab)
combination_strategies.rationale: Combining tarlatamab's DLL3-directed T cell cytotoxicity with PD-1 blockade addresses potential resistance mechanisms. Tarlatamab-activated T cells may upregulate PD-1 as an adaptive immune checkpoint, and pembrolizumab blockade of PD-1 prevents this adaptive resistance. This combination leverages DLL3 as a tumor-specific docking target (tarlatamab) while maintaining T cell effector function through PD-1 release (pembrolizumab), theoretically achieving superior and more durable anti-SCNEC responses than either agent alone in this DLL3-high laryngeal SCNEC.

combination_strategies.name: Lurbinectedin + Irinotecan (Second-Line Combination)
combination_strategies.combination_partner: Irinotecan 75 mg/m2 IV Days 1 and 8, Q3W
combination_strategies.rationale: Lurbinectedin inhibits RNA polymerase II-dependent transcription while irinotecan (topoisomerase I inhibitor) prevents re-ligation of topoisomerase I-mediated DNA nicks during active transcription. This dual targeting of transcription-associated DNA damage creates synthetic lethality in SCLC/SCNEC cells that are transcription-addicted due to their high proliferative rate. Dose-finding Phase 1/2 data shows manageable toxicity with ORR of approximately 28% in R/R SCLC, superior to either agent alone, providing a rationale for second-line doublet therapy in relapsed laryngeal SCNEC after PE.

combination_strategies.name: Vorinostat + Carboplatin/Etoposide (Epigenetic Chemosensitization)
combination_strategies.base_therapy: Vorinostat 400 mg daily x 3 days before and concurrent with chemotherapy
combination_strategies.combination_partner: Carboplatin AUC 5 D1 + Etoposide 100 mg/m2 D1-3, Q3W
combination_strategies.rationale: HDAC inhibition by vorinostat has been shown to sensitize SCLC cells to etoposide by increasing chromatin accessibility at topoisomerase II target sites, enhancing etoposide-induced DNA cleavage. Vorinostat also upregulates pro-apoptotic proteins (BIM, BAX) through histone acetylation and restores expression of epigenetically silenced drug sensitivity genes in TP53-mutated SCNEC. Phase 1 trials in SCLC demonstrated safety of this combination with preliminary signals of activity. This approach is particularly relevant for RB1-deficient, TP53-mutated laryngeal SCNEC where conventional PE resistance may develop through epigenetic mechanisms.

supportive_care.evidence: Crawford et al. NEJM 1991 (N=211 lung cancer patients) and ASCO 2024 Primary Prophylaxis Guidelines mandate G-CSF when febrile neutropenia risk >20%; PE regimen in SCLC exceeds 20% threshold (grade 3-4 neutropenia 60-70%)
supportive_care.benefit: 50% reduction in febrile neutropenia incidence; 40% reduction in hospitalizations for neutropenic fever; enables dose-intensity maintenance (improved OS in SCLC responders)
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A â€” ASCO G-CSF Prophylaxis Guidelines 2024, ESMO Supportive Care 2024

supportive_care.evidence: Hesketh et al. NEJM 2003 (N=521 cisplatin-based chemotherapy) demonstrated triple antiemetic regimen (aprepitant + ondansetron + dexamethasone) reduced acute emesis from 43% to 19%; MASCC/ESMO 2023 guidelines mandate triple therapy for highly emetogenic regimens including cisplatin-based
supportive_care.benefit: 55% reduction in acute emesis rate; 40% improvement in complete response to antiemetic prophylaxis; significant improvement in patient quality of life and treatment compliance
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A â€” MASCC/ESMO Antiemetic Guidelines 2023, ASCO Antiemetics 2024

supportive_care.evidence: Parsons et al. BMJ 2010 systematic review (N=10,000 head and neck/lung cancer patients) demonstrated continued smoking during cancer treatment doubles mucositis severity, increases treatment toxicity 30-40%, and significantly worsens OS; NRT + varenicline combination achieves 25-35% sustained quit rates
supportive_care.benefit: 30-40% reduction in treatment toxicity severity; 25-35% sustained smoking cessation rate; improved OS and reduced second primary cancer risk in smoking-associated malignancies
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A â€” NCCN Smoking Cessation 2025, ESMO Supportive Care 2024, ACS Cancer Treatment Guidelines

supportive_care.evidence: Brown PD et al. JAMA Oncol 2020 (RTOG 0933, N=113 brain metastasis patients) demonstrated HA-WBRT reduced memory decline at 4 months (7% vs 26% with standard WBRT); Gondi et al. Lancet Oncol 2014 confirmed HA approach feasibility and neurocognitive benefit in PCI setting
supportive_care.benefit: 26-50% reduction in neurocognitive decline (memory, executive function) compared to standard PCI; comparable brain metastasis prophylaxis rate while preserving hippocampal neurogenesis
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade B â€” NCCN SCLC Guidelines 2025, ESMO SCLC Guidelines 2024

supportive_care.approach: Nutritional Assessment and Enteral Nutrition Support During CRT
supportive_care.evidence: Langius et al. Clin Nutr 2013 systematic review (N=1,589 HNSCC and thoracic CRT patients) showed early dietitian intervention reduced severe weight loss by 30% and improved treatment completion; ESPEN 2023 guidelines recommend enteral nutrition when oral intake <60% of estimated needs during concurrent CRT
supportive_care.benefit: 30% reduction in severe weight loss (>10% body weight); 20% improvement in treatment completion rate; reduced treatment interruptions due to malnutrition; improved 1-year QoL scores
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A â€” ESPEN Oncology Nutrition 2023, ESMO Supportive Care 2024

supportive_care.approach: CRS Monitoring Protocol for Tarlatamab First Dose
supportive_care.evidence: Ahn MJ et al. NEJM 2023 (DeLLphi-300, N=220) documented 51% CRS rate with tarlatamab; FDA prescribing information mandates 24-hour inpatient monitoring after first dose and each dose escalation; tocilizumab and corticosteroids reduce grade 3+ CRS by 80% when administered promptly
supportive_care.benefit: 80% reduction in grade 3+ CRS severity with premedication and monitoring protocols; prevents tarlatamab-related deaths from severe CRS; enables safe outpatient transition after initial monitoring period
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A (mandatory per FDA label) â€” IMDELLTRA Prescribing Information 2024, NCCN Immunotherapy Toxicity Management 2025
